questions.question: How are CSS custom properties (variables) declared?
questions.explanation: CSS custom properties are declared with a double-hyphen prefix (--), such as --primary-color: blue. They are accessed using the var() function.

questions.question: Why is :root commonly used to define CSS custom properties?
choices: It is the only place custom properties can be defined
It provides document-wide scope since it matches the html element
It provides higher performance than other selectors
Custom properties defined elsewhere are ignored by browsers
questions.explanation: The :root pseudo-class matches the document's root element (html). Defining custom properties there makes them available throughout the entire document via inheritance.

questions.question: What happens when var() references an undefined custom property and no fallback is provided?
choices: The property uses its inherited or initial value
questions.explanation: When a custom property is not defined and no fallback is given, the property using var() becomes invalid at computed-value time. The browser then uses the inherited value or the property's initial value.

questions.question: How do you specify a fallback value in the var() function?
questions.explanation: The var() function accepts an optional second argument separated by a comma that serves as the fallback value: var(--color, blue).

questions.question: CSS custom properties follow the normal ___ rules, meaning a child element can inherit variables defined on a parent.
questions.explanation: Custom properties participate in the CSS cascade just like regular properties. They are inherited by default, so child elements receive values set on ancestor elements.

questions.question: To use a CSS custom property named --spacing in a declaration, write padding: ___.
questions.explanation: The var() function is used to retrieve the value of a custom property. Writing padding: var(--spacing) applies the value of --spacing to the padding property.
